1: Moses speaks to the heads of the Israelite tribes about vow-law. The address 'to the heads of the tribes' (roshei hammatot) signals this as communal law applicable to clan leaders who will adjudicate disputes. Vows (nedarim) and pledges (shebuˁot) were legally binding in all ANE cultures — Akkadian, Ugaritic, and Egyptian texts attest to the seriousness with which they were treated. Breaking a vow was both a legal and a religious offense; Num 30 addresses how vows made by women under male authority structures are to be handled.
2: 'When a man makes a vow to YHWH or swears an oath to bind himself with a pledge, he shall not break his word.' The principle is absolute for men: a vow is unbreakable once made. Deut 23:21–23 reinforces this: 'when you make a vow to the LORD your God, you shall not delay fulfilling it.' Qohelet 5:4–5 applies the same principle: it is better not to vow than to vow and not pay. The verb yeḥallel (profane, break) is a strong term — breaking a vow is not just a civil failure but a desecration.
3: The case of a young woman still living in her father's household: if she makes a vow, its validity is contingent on her father's response. The woman's legal capacity to make binding vows exists but is subject to paternal authority within the household structure. This reflects the ANE patriarchal household as the basic legal unit: a daughter is under her father's legal jurisdiction until transferred to a husband's household.
4: If the father hears and says nothing (heḥerish lah — literally 'keeps silent to/for her'), the vow stands and she must fulfill it. Silence is defined as consent in this legal framework — a principle that appears across ANE legal texts. The Hammurabi Code and Neo-Babylonian legal documents similarly treat the silent party's inaction as ratification of a legal state.
5: If the father nullifies (heniˀ) the vow on the day he hears of it, the vow is void and YHWH will forgive her. The father has a one-day window — the day of hearing — to exercise his authority. After that window, silence has become consent. The forgiveness formula ('YHWH will forgive her') implies the vow was already binding before annulment and that there is some guilt attached to voiding it that requires divine pardon.
6: The case of a woman who makes a vow while under her father's authority and then marries. The pre-marriage vow carries over into the marriage relationship. The husband now has the same authority as the father — he may nullify on the day he hears of it. ANE marriage contracts consistently transferred legal jurisdiction from father to husband; this verse reflects that transfer explicitly in the religious-vow domain.
7: The same silence-as-consent principle applies to the husband: if he hears and is silent on the day, the vow stands. The legal consistency between paternal and marital authority is deliberate — the law's structure is that a woman's vow operates within the authority of whoever heads her household, not that her vow-making capacity is inherently different from a man's.
8: If the husband nullifies the vow after hearing it on the day, he effectively bears the woman's iniquity. The husband's nullification transfers the legal consequences to him — a form of legal substitution. This is a specific case of vicarious responsibility that has no exact ANE parallel: the nullifying party absorbs the obligation rather than simply canceling it.
9: The widow or divorced woman — any vow she makes shall stand. These women have no male household head over them; they have the full legal capacity of a man with respect to vows. The legal autonomy of the widow and divorced woman is explicitly protected: they cannot appeal to a father or husband to void an obligation, but neither can anyone void their vows for them. This is the one case in the chapter where a woman's vow has the same absolute force as a man's.
10: A wife in her husband's household who makes a vow or an oath with a binding pledge is subject to the same day-of-hearing rule. The consistency of the household authority principle across all domestic states (unmarried daughter, newly married, long-married) reflects a systematic legal framework, not ad-hoc rulings.
11: If the husband has consistently been silent day after day about her vows and pledges, they stand — his ongoing silence ratifies them cumulatively. This extends the single-day rule into a cumulative pattern: a husband cannot ignore vows for weeks and then retrospectively nullify them when convenient. The fairness of the law's design protects the woman against arbitrary late revocation.
12: But if the husband nullifies them on the day he hears of them, then whatever she said about vows or pledges shall not stand. The phrase 'her husband has nullified them, and YHWH will forgive her' is the standard formula — the burden transfers to the husband. The pattern of the law is: the one who acts (nullifies) bears the consequence; the one who is acted upon (has their vow voided) is exculpated.
13: The general principle: every vow and every binding oath to deny herself (leˁannot nefesh — to afflict the soul, typically referring to fasting) her husband may either ratify or void. The 'affliction of the soul' is the specific category of self-denial vows — fasting being the primary example (compare Lev 23:27, Yom Kippur; Ps 35:13). These are specifically named because they affect the household — a fasting wife affects food preparation, shared meals, and household rhythms.
14: If the husband says nothing from day to day, he ratifies all her vows and pledges by his silence, because he said nothing to her on the day he heard. The law's logic is equitable from a property-law perspective: vows often involved dedicating household resources; the husband's right to override protects household assets, but the narrow window (one day) prevents abuse.
15: If the husband tries to nullify after the one-day window has passed, he bears her iniquity himself. This is the law's enforcement clause: an attempted late nullification does not succeed in removing the woman's obligation but does transfer moral-legal guilt to the husband. The law protects the woman from being left in a position of having incurred guilt through no fault of her own.
16: Moses delivers these statutes to Israel as YHWH commanded. The chapter functions as a self-contained legal unit on vow-authority within the household. Its position after the festival calendar (ch 28–29) and before the Midian holy war (ch 31) may reflect an editorial logic: the vow chapter closes the liturgical-calendar section before the narrative resumes.
